g.data-narration: Welcome to Domain Name System - the phonebook of the internet that translates names to addresses.
Before DNS, users needed to memorize complex IP addresses like 142.250.190.46 instead of simple names like google.com.
g.text: The Problem: IP Addresses are Hard to Remember
1983: Paul Mockapetris created DNS to replace HOSTS.TXT file

g.data-narration: DNS is a hierarchical distributed database that translates domain names to IP addresses in milliseconds across the globe.
g.text: Domain Name System - A distributed hierarchical naming system for translating
human-readable domain names into numerical IP addresses that computers use.

g.data-narration: DNS uses a hierarchical tree structure: Root (.) → TLD (.com, .org) → Second-Level → Subdomain. This enables distributed authority.
Four types of DNS servers work together: Recursive Resolvers (ISP), Root Servers (13 logical A-M), TLD Servers, and Authoritative Servers.
Recursive queries return complete answers; Iterative queries return best possible answers with referrals to next servers.
g.text: Best answer + referrals to next server

g.data-narration: The complete DNS resolution flow: Client asks Recursive Resolver → Root → TLD → Authoritative → Returns IP to client.
Key DNS records: A maps to IPv4, AAAA to IPv6, CNAME creates aliases, MX specifies mail servers with priority values.
NS delegates zones, SOA contains admin info, PTR reverses IP to domain, TXT is for verification and security like SPF and DKIM.
g.text: ⚠ CNAME cannot coexist with other records for same name • MX priority: lower number = higher priority

g.data-narration: DNS caching occurs at browser, OS, and resolver levels. TTL values determine cache duration - typical range 300s to 86400s (24 hours).
g.text: TTL (Time-To-Live): Lower = faster changes but more queries | Higher = slower propagation but less load

g.data-narration: Dynamic DNS automatically updates DNS records when IP addresses change - essential for home networks with dynamic IPs.
g.text: Traditional DNS requires manual updates • DDNS automates IP changes

g.data-narration: DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to verify DNS response authenticity, protecting against spoofing and cache poisoning attacks.
g.text: Adds cryptographic authentication to DNS responses

g.data-narration: Example: MX 10 mail.example.org has higher priority than MX 20 mail2.example.org - mail servers tried in order.
CNAME creates aliases: shop.example.com points to shopping-example.myshopify.com. Resolver must follow to get final IP.
g.text: ✓ Allows changing service without updating alias DNS • ⚠ Cannot have other records with CNAME

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: DNS hierarchy (Root→TLD→Authoritative), query types (Recursive/Iterative), record types (A/MX/CNAME), caching, and DNSSEC security.
